lines: Settle in. [long pause] Let your body find the shape it wants for sleep. [long pause] Adjust your pillow — tilt it, flatten it, fold it. [long pause] Pull the blanket up to your chin or push it down to your waist. [long pause] Whatever makes you comfortable. [long pause] This is your time. [long pause] No one needs anything from you right now.

Feel the bed beneath you — its support, its give. [long pause] Let your weight drop fully into it, the way snow settles on a branch. [long pause] You don't have to hold yourself up. [long pause] You don't have to hold anything up. [long pause] Not your posture, not your composure, not anyone else's expectations. [long pause] Not until morning.

Notice your hands. [long pause] Where are they? [long pause] [long pause] Let them rest open if that's comfortable. [long pause] Let your fingers uncurl one by one — thumb, index, middle, ring, pinky. [long pause] Each one releasing whatever it was holding today. [long pause] Let your wrists go soft. [long pause] Let your forearms sink into the mattress.

Let your breath slow. [long pause] Breathe in for a count of four... [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] one, two, three, four... [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] and out for eight... [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] one, two, three, four, five, six, seven, eight. [long pause] The long exhale activates the part of your nervous system that says: you're safe. [long pause] You can let go.

Again. [long pause] In for four... [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] one, two, three, four... [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] and out for eight... [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] one, two, three, four, five, six, seven, eight.

Once more. [long pause] In for four... [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] and out for eight. [long pause] Good. [long pause] Let the breathing settle into its own rhythm now. [long pause] You've set it on its way. [long pause] It can take itself from here.

Tonight we practice gratitude. [long pause] Not the performative kind — not the pasting of a smile over a hard day, not the guilt of knowing you should feel thankful when you don't. [long pause] The settled kind. [long pause] The honest kind. [long pause] The kind that looks clearly at what is — the good and the difficult — and says: even so, there is something here worth noticing.

The apostle Paul writes to the Thessalonians: "Rejoice always, pray continually, give thanks in all circumstances; for this is God's will for you in Christ Jesus." First Thessalonians five, sixteen through eighteen.

In all circumstances. [long pause] That's a hard teaching. [long pause] Paul isn't asking you to be thankful for suffering. [long pause] He's not saying paste gratitude over your pain like a bandage. [long pause] He's pointing to something subtler — that even within the difficult, there's a thread of grace running through, if you're willing to slow down enough to find it.
In all circumstances. [long pause] In them. [long pause] Not for them. [long pause] That's an important distinction. [long pause] Hold on to it.

We'll move through three kinds of gratitude tonight. [long pause] A person. [long pause] Your body. [long pause] And a small, overlooked moment from today. [long pause] Then we'll walk slowly through a section that's harder — gratitude within the difficult. [long pause] And finally, we'll let that gratitude settle deep into your body like warmth, carrying you down toward sleep.

Bring to mind one person who showed you some form of kindness recently. [long pause] It doesn't have to be dramatic. [long pause] It doesn't have to be a grand gesture. [long pause] Maybe a colleague who listened when they didn't have to. [long pause] A friend who sent a message at the right moment. [long pause] A stranger who held a door, or smiled, or let you go first in line. [long pause] Someone whose presence made your day slightly lighter, slightly more bearable.

See their face. [long pause] What did they do, specifically? [long pause] [long pause] How did it feel to receive that? [long pause] [long pause] Was there a moment — even a brief one — where you felt seen? [long pause] [long pause] Helped? [long pause] [long pause] Simply less alone in the world?
[long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause]

Stay with that feeling. [long pause] Don't rush past it. [long pause] Gratitude isn't just a thought — it's a physical warmth. [long pause] A loosening in the chest. [long pause] A softening around the eyes. [long pause] You can feel it in your body if you stay with it long enough. [long pause] Let it spread.

Silently, in whatever words feel natural, thank God for that person. [long pause] Not a formal prayer. [long pause] Not something you'd say in church. [long pause] Just a quiet, honest acknowledgment. [long pause] Thank you for them. [long pause] Thank you that they exist. [long pause] Thank you that they crossed my path.

Now let that person's face fade gently, and turn your attention inward — to your body. [long pause] This body that carried you through another day. [long pause] This body that you so often take for granted, or criticize, or push past its limits. [long pause] Tonight, we offer it thanks.

Start with your lungs. [long pause] They breathed without being asked. [long pause] All day. [long pause] All night. [long pause] Right now, without any instruction from you, they fill and empty, fill and empty. [long pause] Twenty thousand breaths today — each one happening automatically, faithfully, without complaint. [long pause] You never had to think about a single one.

Your heart. [long pause] One hundred thousand beats today. [long pause] Each one a gift you didn't earn. [long pause] Each one pushing blood through sixty thousand miles of vessels — to your brain, your fingers, your toes. [long pause] A whole infrastructure of life humming beneath your awareness, keeping you alive while you worried about emails and deadlines and things that will barely matter next week.

Your hands. [long pause] What did they hold today? [long pause] [long pause] A cup of coffee. [long pause] A steering wheel. [long pause] A phone. [long pause] A child's hand. [long pause] A pen. [long pause] A doorknob. [long pause] They created, they carried, they reached out, they typed, they cooked, they cleaned. [long pause] Thank them for their service. [long pause] They've earned their rest.

Your feet. [long pause] They carried you from room to room, from task to task, from morning to night. [long pause] They bore your full weight without complaint, step after step after step. [long pause] Feel them now — still and warm at the end of the bed. [long pause] They've walked you home. [long pause] They're done for today.

Your eyes. [long pause] All the things you saw today — color and light and faces and words and the sky and the ordinary miracles of a world that is constantly, extravagantly visual. [long pause] Now they're closed, and even that is a gift — the ability to close your eyes and shut out the world for a while. [long pause] To choose darkness. [long pause] To choose rest.

Now, one small moment from today that held some goodness. [long pause] Something easily overlooked. [long pause] It doesn't have to be significant. [long pause] A taste — maybe that first sip of something warm this morning, the way it spread through your chest. [long pause] A color — the way the light hit a wall, or a leaf, or a face, and made something ordinary briefly beautiful. [long pause] A sound — birdsong through a window, laughter from another room, a song that caught you off guard. [long pause] A sensation — cool air on your face, warm water on your hands, the weight of a blanket at the end of the day.
[long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause]

Stay with it. [long pause] Don't rush past it to find something more impressive. [long pause] This is the practice — to notice that the ordinary is saturated with grace, that the mundane is shot through with mercy, if you slow down enough to see it. [long pause] The small moment is not less valuable than the grand one. [long pause] It may be more so.

Now here is the stretch. [long pause] Was there something hard today? [long pause] [long pause] Something that didn't go the way you wanted? [long pause] [long pause] A frustration. [long pause] A disappointment. [long pause] A conflict. [long pause] A loss. [long pause] Something that hurt, even if only a little.

You don't have to be grateful for the pain. [long pause] That's not what this is. [long pause] But can you find one thing within the hard moment that carried a seed of good? [long pause] [long pause] Maybe it taught you something about yourself that you needed to learn. [long pause] Maybe someone showed up for you in the middle of it. [long pause] Maybe you discovered a resilience you didn't know you had. [long pause] Maybe the hard thing clarified something — what really matters, what you actually value, where your limits are.

If nothing comes, that's fine. [long pause] Some days the gratitude for the difficult takes weeks, or months, or years. [long pause] You don't have to force it. [long pause] The willingness to look — just the willingness — is enough. [long pause] You can set the hard thing down tonight and trust that understanding will come when you're ready.

God, thank you for this day. [long pause] Not because it was perfect — it wasn't. [long pause] Thank you because it was mine, and you were in it. [long pause] Thank you for the person who showed me kindness I didn't earn. [long pause] Thank you for this body that works so faithfully without my asking. [long pause] Thank you for the small, beautiful moments I almost missed and the ones I did miss — I trust they were there. [long pause] And for the hard parts — I don't understand them yet. [long pause] But I trust you to bring good from them in time. [long pause] You always have. [long pause] I lay it all down now. [long pause] Amen.

Now let gratitude become something physical. [long pause] Let it become warmth. [long pause] A deep, pleasant warmth that starts at the crown of your head and will move slowly, slowly, all the way down to your toes. [long pause] Let it become heaviness. [long pause] A settling. [long pause] A sinking. [long pause] The body's way of saying: it is well.

Bring your attention to the top of your head. [long pause] Feel a warmth gathering there — gentle and diffuse, like a hand resting lightly on your crown. [long pause] Let it spread slowly across your scalp. [long pause] Feel the tension in the tiny muscles there releasing — tension you didn't even know you were holding. [long pause] The warmth moves across your scalp like warm water being poured slowly over your head.

The warmth moves to your forehead. [long pause] Feel the muscles smoothing out, the lines of concentration dissolving, the furrow between your brows becoming flat and open. [long pause] Your forehead becomes wide and still, like a calm horizon.

The warmth flows down around your eyes. [long pause] Your eyelids grow heavier — so heavy they couldn't open if they wanted to. [long pause] The tiny muscles around your eyes, the ones that squint and focus and strain all day, softening completely. [long pause] All the watching and reading and scanning of the day... [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] released.

The warmth reaches your jaw. [long pause] This is where so many of us hold the day — the clenched teeth during a tense conversation, the bitten tongue when we held back words, the tight muscles of swallowed frustration. [long pause] Let your jaw hang open. [long pause] Let your tongue go slack. [long pause] Let your cheeks soften. [long pause] Let all of that tension drain out like water through sand.

Down into your neck. [long pause] The muscles on both sides of your throat, the tendons that tightened every time you braced against stress... [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] softening now. [long pause] Unwinding. [long pause] Your head feels heavy on the pillow. [long pause] Deliciously heavy. [long pause] Let it be heavy. [long pause] Let the pillow do its job.

The warmth pours into your shoulders. [long pause] These faithful, overworked workers. [long pause] They've been carrying weight all day — physical weight, emotional weight, the invisible weight of responsibility and worry. [long pause] Feel them drop. [long pause] An inch. [long pause] Then another inch. [long pause] Lower than you thought they could go. [long pause] Lower than they've been in months. [long pause] You are off duty. [long pause] Whatever you were carrying can wait until morning.

Down through your arms. [long pause] Upper arms heavy against the bed, like they're filled with warm sand. [long pause] Elbows resting. [long pause] Forearms loose and still. [long pause] Wrists limp. [long pause] Hands open. [long pause] Fingers curled softly inward, holding nothing at all. [long pause] Not a phone. [long pause] Not a pen. [long pause] Not a worry. [long pause] Nothing.

The warmth fills your chest now. [long pause] Feel your ribcage expanding and contracting with each easy, unhurried breath. [long pause] Your heart beating its quiet rhythm underneath — steady, grateful, faithful. [long pause] No rushing. [long pause] No pounding. [long pause] Just the calm, measured work of a heart that knows its job.

Down into your belly. [long pause] The soft center of your body. [long pause] The place where anxiety lives, where the knot forms during stress. [long pause] Let it be soft. [long pause] Truly soft. [long pause] No holding in, no bracing, no sucking in. [long pause] Just... [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] soft. [long pause] Rising and falling like a sleeping child's belly. [long pause] Innocent of the day's worries. [long pause] Open and round and at peace.

The warmth moves through your hips. [long pause] Your pelvis settles deeper into the mattress. [long pause] Your lower back releases whatever it was gripping — the bracing, the holding, the tension of hours spent sitting or standing or carrying.

Down through your thighs. [long pause] Heavy, heavy thighs. [long pause] The large muscles that powered you through the day — standing, walking, climbing, braking — all of them letting go completely now. [long pause] Melting into the bed like warm butter.

Your knees. [long pause] Your calves. [long pause] The muscles that tightened on stairs and sidewalks, that bore your weight step after step... [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] dissolving now into warmth and heaviness.

Your ankles. [long pause] The tops of your feet. [long pause] The arches. [long pause] The soles of your feet — warm and tingling slightly, like they're being held in warm hands. [long pause] Your toes, one by one, releasing. [long pause] Big toe. [long pause] Second toe. [long pause] Third. [long pause] Fourth. [long pause] Little toe. [long pause] Each one letting go of the ground it stood on today.

Your entire body is now warm and heavy with gratitude. [long pause] From the crown of your head to the tips of your toes. [long pause] Not the frantic kind. [long pause] The settled kind. [long pause] The kind that doesn't perform or prove anything. [long pause] The kind that simply says... [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] it is well.
It is well with my soul.
Rest now, held in the goodness of this day.
[long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause]
